text: The surgeon begins dissection over the sacral promontory, carefully maintaining hemostasis while exposing the area for sutures. The posterior holy plane is identified during this meticulous dissection.

The surgeon performs a full posterior and anterior dissection down to the pelvic floor, along with a nearly complete right lateral dissection, while leaving the left side untouched. The anatomy appears distorted due to chronic prolapse, but the surgeon uses tension to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon uses a suture to retract the bladder flap, revealing the distortion caused by the prolapse. The right lateral dissection has extended anteriorly, and the surgeon prepares to enter the deep anterior cul-de-sac to begin the anterior dissection.

The surgeon carefully navigates the dissection plane, hugging the back of the vagina to avoid injury to the rectum or vagina. Mesorectal fat is kept with the rectum, but as the dissection progresses lower, the anterior mesorectal fat diminishes.

The surgeon completes the posterior dissection around the sacral promontory, focusing on achieving hemostasis on the bone using bipolar cautery to control bleeding. Large stitches are placed through the lateral stalks using Tuo-Tychron with a larger needle.
The surgeon performs a repair following a thorough anterior and full posterior dissection, preparing to take a second bite with the suture to ensure durability of the repair.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap to prevent the small bowel from entering the dissected space or forming adhesions. The peritoneum retracts, revealing the proximity of the suturing site to the right ureter, which the surgeon checks frequently during the closure.
